making an order substituting Mars CFI DAC as the plaintiff in these proceedings and granting
Mars CFI DAC possession of the subject property. Further, having regard to the matters raised
by the first named defendant, I do not consider that he has established any defence or any
arguable grounds of defence which would warrant the application for possession being
transferred to plenary hearing.
111. Insofar as any ancillary arguments are being maintained in this respect, I do not consider that
it is necessary that the various deponents in both the possession application and the substitution
application be made available for cross-examination in order to determine these applications.
112. The underlying proceedings seek to recover possession of the subject property. As set out earlier
in this judgment, I am satisfied that Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank as seller and Mars CFI
DAC as buyer, executed a Deed of Transfer whereby Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank
transferred all its rights, legal rights, title, interest, estate, and entitlement, past and present, in
and under the Underlying Loans, the Mortgage Assets, and the Finance Documents which
included the facility and the security which are the subject of these proceedings to Mars CFI
DAC and that the Transfer Deed was dated 2nd October 2023.
113. Similar arguments to those raised by the first named defendant were canvassed in AIB plc and
Everyday Finance DAC v Doran [2025] IEHC 515. In that case, the High Court (Owens J.)
reviewed the applicable case law, including the decision of the High Court (Charleton J.) in
42

IRBC v Cambourne Investments Incorporated and Others [2012] IEHC 262, [2014] 4 I.R. 54,
(referred to earlier) and the authorities referred to in that judgment and observed at para. 213
that “Non-satisfaction of a proof which the bank was contractually entitled to insist on as a
pre-condition to permitting draw-down of Thomas Doran’s loans did not necessarily result in
automatic termination of the contracts embodied in the facility letters: see O’Connor v Coady
([2004] IESC 54) [2004] 3 I.R. 271 per Geoghegan J. at p. 278 para. [11]” and, therefore,
non-compliance with preconditions does not automatically terminate a contract.
114. In Lakeland Agri Ltd v Hand [2021] IEHC 13 the High Court (Simons J.) addressed, on the
facts of that case, the question of the application of the subset of the jurisprudence on summary
summons proceedings which addresses the distinction between a cross-claim which amounts
to a defence, and one which is merely a counterclaim and referred to McGrath v O’Driscoll
[2006] IEHC 195; [2007] 1 I.L.R.M. 203, and Moohan v S. & R. Motors (Donegal) Ltd v
Bradley Construction [2007] IEHC 435; [2008] 3 I.R. 650.
115. At para. 18 of his judgment, Simons J. stated that the overall approach to be taken to a case
involving a cross-claim was summarised by Clarke J. (as he then was) in Moohan v S. & R.
Motors (Donegal) Ltd v Bradley Construction [2007] IEHC 435; [2008] 3 I.R. 650, at pp.
656/657, as follows:
“(a) it is firstly necessary to determine whether the defendant has established a defence
as such to the plaintiff’s claim. In order for the asserted cross-claim to amount to a
defence as such, it must arguably give rise to a set off in equity and must, thus, stem
from the same set of circumstances as give rise to the claim but also arise in
43

circumstances where, on the basis of the defendant’s case, it would not be inequitable
to allow the asserted set off;
(b) if and to the extent that a prima facie case for such a set off arises, the defendant
will be taken to have established a defence to the proceedings and should be given
liberty to defend the entire (or an appropriate proportion of) the claim (or have same,
in a case such as that with which I am concerned, referred to arbitration);
(c) if the cross-claim amounts to an independent claim, then judgment should be entered
on the claim but the question of whether execution of such judgment should be stayed
must be determined in the discretion of the court by reference to the principles set out
by Kingsmill Moore J. in Prendergast v. Biddle (Unreported, Supreme Court, 31st July,
1957).”
116. On the facts of that case, Simons J. had further regard to the judgment of Kingsmill Moore J.
in Prendergast v Biddle (Unreported, Supreme Court, 31st July 1957) and held that the nature
of the counterclaim sought to be advanced by the defendant in that case was simply “too
vague”. The Court granted judgment which order did not preclude the defendant from pursuing,
in separate proceedings, any claim for damages arising out of the alleged breach of the haulage
services contract which was at issue in that case, as it amounted to an independent claim. In
AIB plc and Everyday Finance DAC v Doran [2025] IEHC 515, the High Court (Owens J.)
observed at para. 69 that a contract may be treated by a party who is not in breach as terminated
where that breach which is sufficiently serious to justify refusal of further performance. Where
a contract has been substantially performed this is not possible and the remedy of the party not
in breach is confined to damages.
44

117. A counterclaim is entirely independent of the actual claim, which in this case is a possession
application in the circumstances set out in this judgment. In this case, there is no dispute but
that the defendants received the loan in 2008, used the sums that were loaned and had a
contractual obligation to repay those sums but that within a year of taking out the mortgage and
receiving the loan, the defendants had defaulted. The claims now alleged by the first named
defendant in response to the substitution and possession applications are not made out.
118. For a period of approximately 17 years, the defendants have had the benefit of moneys loaned
to them and failed to make the contractual repayments as per the loan agreement. The loan was
called-in on 11th March 2009 when the defendants were written to and informed that despite
previous requests and reminders, they had failed to meet the instalment repayments due on their
home loan. The letter called upon the defendants to repay the entire debt within 10 days of the
letter and stated that in default of receipt of such payment together with the interest due,
proceedings for recovery of the mortgaged property would be instituted against the defendants
without further notice.
119. By letters dated 16th April 2009, sent to both defendants, reference was made to the previous
correspondence dated 11th March 2009 demanding full repayment of the total amount due on
foot of the mortgage and the defendants’ failure and refusal to discharge the said sums. The
letter to the defendants dated 16th April 2009 demanded possession of the subject property.
Thereafter, and at all material times, the defendants were written to and notified of the
increasing level of indebtedness by reference to statements of account.
120. It has been established at the de novo hearing of these applications over two days in October
2025 that Mars CFI DAC, as the applicant and moving party, is the registered owner of the
45

Mortgage and Charge over the property at Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan, County Westmeath,
comprised in Folio 21560F of the Register of Freeholders, County Westmeath and more
particularly described as including a plot of ground being part of the Townland of
CLONYHAGUE and barony of MOYCASHEL shown as Plan(s) A738W, edged RED on the
Registry Map (OS MAP Ref(s) 32R/12, 32/16) as per the statement of the law set out in Bank
of Ireland Mortgage Bank v Cody [2021] IESC 26, [2021] 2 I.R. 381 (the Supreme Court, Baker
J.), ss. 62, 62(6), 62(7) of the Registration of Title Act 1964, s. 1 of the Land and Conveyancing
Law Reform Act 2013 and the decision of the High Court (Woulfe J.) in Start Mortgages DAC
v Ryan and Ryan [2021] IEHC 719.
121. The Civil Bill for Possession and Special Indorsement of Claim issued on 8th June 2010 is in
compliance with the Rules of the Circuit Court (S.I. 261/2009), including O. 5B of the CCR.
122. The Register of Freeholders for the County of Westmeath confirms that Mars CFI DAC is the
owner of the Mortgage and Charge on the Folio 21560F which at Entry No. (1) described “A
plot of ground being part of the Townland of CLONYHAGUE and barony of MOYCASHEL
shown as Plan(s) A738W, edged RED on the Registry Map (OS MAP Ref(s) 32R/12, 32/16…),
at Entry No.(2) refers to 15-May-2008 D2008NL030462B-EDMUND SEERY of Cloneyheigue,
Kilbeggan, County Westmeath and ANN SEERY of Cloneyheigue, Kilbeggan, County
Westmeath are full owners, at Entry No. (4) refers to 04-APR-2008 D2008NL019972E Charge
for present and future advances repayable with interest. BANK OF IRELAND MORTGAGE
BANK is owner of this charge. Note: The ownership of this charge has been transferred. See
Entry No.7, and at Entry No. (7) refers to 23-NOV-2023 D2023LR179965C MARS CAPITAL
FINANCE IRELAND DESIGNATED ACTIVITY COMPANY is the owner of the charge
registered at Enry No.4.”
46

123. Accordingly, s. 31 of the Registration of Title Act 1964 provides that the Register is conclusive
evidence of any right, privilege, appurtenance or burden appearing on the register.
124. I am, therefore, satisfied that Mars CFI DAC, as the party moving the application for possession
of the subject property, is the registered owner of the subject Mortgage and Charge, that its
right to possession has arisen and has become exercisable and that it is the appropriate plaintiff
who is entitled to maintain these proceedings and is thereby entitled to an order of possession
of the subject property: see the decision of the Court of Appeal in Tanager DAC v Kane [2018]
IECA 352 [2019] 1 I.R. 385, Start Mortgages DAC v Ryan and Ryan [2021] IEHC 719, Bank
of Ireland Mortgage Bank v Cody [2021] IESC 26, [2021] 2 I.R. 381.
125. The defendants took out the initial loan in 2008 and went into default in their repayments in
2009 and the right to possession of the property vested in the owner of the Mortgage and Charge
has arisen and has become exercisable. Extremely modest repayments were made and the
indebtedness increased year on year over a 17-year period and including the period of these
proceedings. The defendants have not in fact disputed that they are unable to address their
indebtedness. As the owner of the loans, Mars CFI DAC are entitled to be substituted for the
Bank and are further entitled to an order of possession of the subject property.
126. I therefore dismiss the first named defendant’s appeals against the order of the Circuit Court
made on 6th July 2018 in Record No: 2018/269CA and the order of the Circuit Court made on
12th November 2024 in Record No: 2024/264CA.
47

127. I shall make orders substituting Mars Capital Finance Ireland Designated Activity Company as
the plaintiff in the proceedings in Record No: 2018/269CA and Record No: 2024/264CA
against the defendants and I shall further order that Mars Capital Finance Ireland Designated
Activity Company do recover from the defendants possession of ALL THAT AND THOSE the
property known as Clonyhague (otherwise Cloneyheigue), Kilbeggan, County Westmeath (“the
property”) comprised in Folio 21560F of the Register of Freeholders for the County of
Westmeath.
PROPOSED ORDERS
128. Accordingly, subject to discussing the precises terms of the orders with the parties, I propose
to make the following orders:
(i) An order pursuant to O. 22, r. 4 of the Circuit Court Rules substituting Mars Capital
Finance Ireland Designated Activity Company as the Plaintiff in the proceedings in
Record No: 2018/269CA and (if required) Record No: 2024/264CA (and, insofar as is
necessary, directing (and/or deeming) that the within applications are carried on (and/or
were conducted) as between Mars Finance (Ireland) Designated Activity Company (in
lieu of Bank of Ireland Mortgage Bank) as Plaintiff and against the Defendants);
(ii) An order that Mars Capital Finance Ireland Designated Activity Company do recover
from the Defendants possession of ALL THAT AND THOSE the property known as
Clonyhague (otherwise Cloneyheigue), Kilbeggan, County Westmeath (“the property”)
comprised in Folio 21560F of the Register of Freeholders for the County of Westmeath.
48

129. I shall discuss with the parties the terms of these proposed orders (including the period of any
stay on the possession order) and any ancillary or consequential matters which arise, (including
whether an order is required pursuant to O. 36, r. 10 of the CCR) and the question of costs.
130. I shall put the matter in for mention on Wednesday 25th February 2026 at 10:30am.
CONLETH BRADLEY
13th February 2026
49

title: Burns -v- John J. Quinn and Co. LLP. and Ors
text:
APPROVED [2026] IEHC 77
THE HIGH COURT
H.P.2025.0002270
BETWEEN
GERRY BURNS
PLAINTIFF
AND
JOHN J. QUINN & CO. LPP. AND PAULINE BRADY AND MONIKA OSAGIE
DEFENDANTS
JUDGMENT of Mr. Justice Nolan delivered on the 13th day of February 2026
Introduction
1. This is an application to dismiss the Plaintiff’s claim pursuant to Order 19 Rule 28 (1)
of the Rules of the Superior Courts (“the Rules”) or alternatively pursuant to the inherent
jurisdiction of the court on the basis that the case discloses no reasonable cause of action and
is bound to fail.
2. The second motion before the Court is that of the Plaintiff, in which he seeks various
reliefs including inspection of the First and Second Named Defendants (“the Solicitors”) files,

various injunctions and other reliefs, but it seems to me that that motion is only relevant if the
Solicitors are unsuccessful in their application.
Background
3. The Plaintiff and his wife purchased a property at 15 Bruce Manor, Arva, County Cavan
(“the property”), sometime in the early 2000s. For that purpose, he took out a loan with
Permanent TSB (“PTSB”). He alleges that on the 1st of February 2019, PTSB unlawfully
securitised and transferred his mortgage to Start Mortgages DAC (“Start”). He says that PTSB
failed to obtain his consent to enable the lawful transfer of the loan account and therefore the
transfer is unlawful due to breaches of the General Data Protection Regulation (“GDPR”). This,
he alleges, gives rise to legal difficulties.
4. The consequences, for the purposes of this litigation, relate to his allegation that any
processing by any controller of information relating to the loan account (in this case his name,
the name of the property and any description of it) is unlawful.
5. Start appointed receivers with power of sale over the property (Ken Fennell and James
Anderson). On the 31st of May 2024, Start transferred the mortgage to Mars Capital Finance
Ireland DAC (“Mars”) and by deed of novation re-appointed the same receivers.
6. In September 2024, the property was put up for auction online using the Bid X1
platform. Third parties, for whom the Solicitors act, were successful in their bid and the
Solicitors carried out enquires and assisted in the contract for sale. They did the normal acts
that any solicitor acting for a purchaser of property would do, such as carrying out enquires,
assisting in the conclusion of the contract for sale, transferring the purchase monies and
ensuring that all Stamp Duty was paid.

7. As the conveyance triggered a first registration requirement it was necessary for the
purchasers to complete a first registration with Tailte Éireann, which again the Solicitors
assisted and indeed are still assisting with.
8. The Third Named Defendant is a tenant of the purchasers and is presently residing in
the property.
9. The Plaintiff says that all this legal activity is in breach of his GDPR rights and
consequently is illegal.
Associated Proceedings
10. These are not the only proceedings he has brought, as the affidavits filed show. He also
issued another set of proceedings before these, on the 12th of September 2024, just before the
sale by auction online. These proceedings are against everyone involved in the sale of the
property, including the receivers, solicitors acting for the receivers, the auction house, as well
as Start, Mars and Tailte Éireann. He admits that he has failed to progress those proceedings,
focusing instead on these proceedings, notwithstanding that he alleges that these proceedings
are all about a breach of his GDPR rights.
His Stated Cause of Action
11. The Plaintiff’s case is that Start had no authority to deal with his loan in the first place
because PTSB did not obtain his consent to the original transfer. Thereafter, every transaction
was tainted with that alleged illegality, mainly because of breaches of the provisions of the
GDPR legislation. Therefore, he says the transfer of his loan account was unlawful, as was the
subsequent sale. He alleges that the First and Second Named Defendants, as solicitors should
have known that. This gives rise to his allegations of money laundering.

12. While his affidavit deals with various issues relating to how he found out that the
property had become rented, a subsequent complaint to an Garda Síochána and his interaction
with an Garda Síochána, these matters do not add anything further to his case.
13. On the 20th of April 2025, he issued a plenary summons seeking multiple declarations,
amounting to 40, relating to breaches of his constitutional, human and fundamental rights as
well as breaches of the GDPR legislation. He has also served a Statement of Claim seeking a
further 11 reliefs, making a total of 51 reliefs and declarations.
14. I think at this point it is worth commenting upon the pleadings. The plenary summons
runs to 41 paragraphs, while the statement of claim runs to 77 paragraphs, before one gets to
the reliefs. They read relatively cogently, but in fact say little or nothing of relevance. The
pleadings do not set out a factual basis in which to seek the reliefs sought. Instead, they recite
the facts as set out above, allege an unlawful sale and plead various breaches of the GDPR, but
not how the known facts relate to the alleged breaches.
15. I will turn to those regulations below, but it is patently clear to me that the Plaintiff did
not draft these documents. He admitted as much to me in court. He said he had the assistance
of a “GDPR expert”. I permitted him the assistance of a McKenzie friend who, in essence,
dictated his submissions to court. He sought liberty for the McKenzie friend to address the
court, which I refused.
16. It is not the role of a McKenzie friend to draft documents, legal submissions or purport
to act as a legal adviser, since that is prohibited, by criminal sanction, pursuant to both the
Solicitors Act 1954 (as amended) and the Legal Services Regulation Act 2015 (as amended). I
would be concerned that litigants such as the Plaintiff may be asked to pay for such advice.
That too is a criminal offence. Persons who seek this advice should act very carefully.

The Application
17. In her grounding affidavit, the Second Named Defendant sets out the history of the
purchase and subsequent sale of the property. There is no dispute between the parties as to the
basic facts as set out above.
18. She notes that at no stage has her firm acted for or against the Plaintiff. They have never
corresponded with him other than in this litigation. They did not prepare or publish the legal
pack in relation to the auction. She says that this could not amount to a breach of his GDPR
rights, but even if there was some use of his personal data, it was proportionate and necessary
for the legitimate purpose of the Solicitors and their clients, the purchasers.
19. She criticises the Plaintiff’s claim that she or her firm have engaged in some sort of
criminal activity, amounting to money laundry. She calls upon the Plaintiff to withdraw such
unsubstantiated and professionally damaging allegations, which he has not done.
20. In my view she is perfectly correct on this point. The courts have repeatedly criticised
the making of unfounded allegations of criminality. (See O’Hara v Ireland & Ors [2023] IEHC
268, O’Moore J. Carthy v Ireland & Ors [2024] IEHC 490, Ulster Bank DAC v McDonagh
(No. 3) [2024] IEHC 609 and Mullins v Ireland [2022] IEHC 296).
21. In this case not only does he make very serious unfounded allegations against the
Solicitors, but he also criticises counsel and Mr Justice Cregan, the list judge, for abusing his
GDPR rights, in circumstances where Mr Justice Cregan simply assigned the case to this court.
On that basis, I asked, if by hearing the application was the Court abusing his GDPR rights, to
which strangely he said no.

Art. 5 and Art. 6 of the GPDR
22. The purpose of the GDPR is to create a strong, consistent, and modern data-protection
framework across the EU. In simple terms, it sets out how organisations must handle personal
data and what rights individuals have over their own information. GDPR ensures that people’s
personal information is processed lawfully, fairly, and transparently, collected only for specific
and explicit purposes and limited to what is necessary.
23. It strengthens individuals’ power by giving them certain rights, all of which must be
balanced against a legitimate need for that information. To that end, Article 79 allows that a
data subject can take legal action where there is evidence that their data rights have been
infringed. However, such action must be based upon evidence, not on an impression or a
supposition. Mr. Geoghegan BL for the Solicitors says it does not permit an audit of a data
controllers files to see if there has been an infringement.
24. The two important articles for the purposes of this application are Article 5 and 6.
Article 5 defines the ethical and operational standards for data processing, while Article 6
outlines the legal bases that must apply for any processing of personal data to be lawful.
25. Article 5 states that personal data shall be processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent
manner. It should be collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and should be
adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary, in relation to that purpose. It should be
accurately kept for no longer than is necessary and kept securely.
26. Article 6 states that processing is lawful, if at least one of the following applies;
a. the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one
or more specific purposes;
b. processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is
party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a
contract;

c. processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller
is subject;
d. processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of
another natural person;
e. processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest
or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller.
f. processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the
controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the
interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require
protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child.
27. Art 6 (1) b. c. e and f are particularly relevant in this case.
28. In Case C-252/21, Meta Platforms v Bundeskartellamt (“Meta Platforms”) established
a legitimate interest test, also known as a three-stage test for data controllers to determine if
their use of data is lawful.
29. These are the Purpose Test — is there a legitimate interest? The controller must identify
a specific, lawful, real, and clearly defined interest for the processing (e.g., fraud prevention,
network security, product improvement).
30. The Necessity Test — is the processing necessary? The controller must determine
whether the processing is essential to achieve the stated purpose. If the same outcome can be
achieved with less intrusive means, the processing is not necessary.
31. Finally, the Balancing Test — do the data subject’s rights override the controller’s
interest? The controller must weigh their interest against the individual’s rights and freedoms,
reasonable expectations, and potential harm or intrusion. If the individual’s rights outweigh the

interest, legitimate interests cannot be relied upon. It is for the national courts to determine the
issue, not the data subject, in this case, the Plaintiff.
The Legal Principles Applicable to Strike Out
32. The legal principles applicable to an application to strike out pursuant to Order 19 Rule
28 (1) of the Rules are well known. (See Aer Rianta v Ryanair [2004] IESC 23, [2004] 1 IR
506). It is a jurisdiction which should be used sparingly and only in the clearest of cases. It is
not there to prevent hardship to a Defendant from defending a case but simply to prevent an
abuse of process. The onus is at all times, on the Defendant. The court should be cognisant of
the fact that the threshold for a Plaintiff to successfully defend such a motion is not a prima
facia case but a statable case. (see Scotchstone Capital Fund Limited v. Ireland [2022] IECA
23, Lopes v. Minister for Justice and Equality [2014] 2 IR 301). The default position is that the
proceedings should go to trial and the motion should only be brought where it is clear there is
no real risk of injustice in so doing. Crucially the court must be satisfied that no amendment or
discovery could change the outcome of the case and that the case is fundamentally incapable
of success.
33. Two other points of note and that is, if there is no meaningful dispute as to the relevant
facts, it is open to a court to dismiss a claim if it simply cannot succeed as a matter of law.
Secondly even if there is some tangential or irrelevant dispute this should not stop a court from
proceeding to dismiss it.
34. In this case there is no dispute as to the relevant facts.

Discussion The Duties of a Solicitor
35. The first port of call in this case relates to the obligations of a solicitor to act for a client
in any conveyance. They are employed under a contract of retainer. Pursuant to their
obligations they are obliged to carry out certain investigations. These involve investigating the
root and chain of title, carrying out searches, dealing with requisitions on title, drafting deeds
of conveyance to complete the transaction and much more.
36. Not only is this a contractual duty, it is a statutory duty, pursuant to Section 58 of the
Solicitors Act 1954 and Section 50 of the Legal Regulation Services Act 2015. Naturally in the
course of such a conveyance the name of the previous owner, this case the Plaintiff and his
wife, will be referred to. It is of note that the contract for sale of the property was in the name
of the Plaintiff and his wife, albeit through the receivers appointed.
37. Pursuant to Section 25 of the Registration of Title Act 1964, it is mandatory to register
the ownership of land and pursuant to Section 62 of the Land and Conveyancing Reform Act
of 2009, a legal interest in land may only be created or conveyed by deed, which is normally
drafted by a solicitor.
38. As if that were not enough authority, it would be professional negligence for a solicitor
not to continue their retainer and in certain circumstances go beyond, in the interests of their
client. There is no doubt whatsoever that the role of a solicitor requires the use of a lot of
information.
Discussion and Decision
39. Turning to the Plaintiff’s case, he alleges that by using his name, the address and
description of the property, in circumstances where he is disputing the right of the holder of

the mortgage of the property to sell it to a third part, is in some way illegal and on that basis
the use of his data is unlawful.
40. In my view that is an unstable case. The use of his data was and is not unlawful because
it falls squarely with the parameters of Article 6(1)(b), in that it is necessary for the performance
of a contract which the data subject was party, namely the contract for sale, albeit through the
receivers appointed. It falls within Article 6(1)(c), in that it was and is necessary for compliance
with a legal obligation to which the controller (the Solicitors) is subject, namely the duty they
owe their clients. It falls within Article 6(1)(e), in that it was necessary for the performance of
a task carried out in the public interest, such as the registration of title and finally Article 6
(1)(f), in that it was necessary for the purpose of the legitimate interest of the controller, The
Solicitors, acting for their clients.
41. While he (or his McKenzie friend) argues that the case is all about alleged breaches of
data, it is clear that is not the case. It is in fact about an alternative purpose, namely, to prevent
the conclusion of the sale of the property. Why otherwise would he have joined the Third
Named Defendant, namely the tenant residing in the property. She is neither a data controller
nor somebody who has had any access to his data, to any significant degree. She was joined to
the proceedings because he is trying to interfere with the conclusion of the sale.
42. The courts will not allow proceedings which have an alternative purpose or improper
purpose to continue. Such proceedings are an abuse of process.
43. In Sean Quinn Group Ltd v an Bord Pleanála [2001] 1 IR 505, Quirke J. found that
Judicial review proceedings brought challenging a planning permission had an alternative
purpose. His words ring true in this case.
44. He said: -
“In the instant case I am satisfied on the evidence that the proceedings herein have been
commenced by the plaintiff in a cynical, calculated and unscrupulous fashion for the

sole purpose of seeking a commercial advantage over its competitor Lagan. I am
further satisfied that the purpose for which these proceedings have been instituted has
not been to redress a wrong or a grievance, to right an injustice, to ensure compliance
by Lagan and by other potential developers with provisions of national or international
legislation, to ensure the proper and lawful planning and development of any particular
area or for any other commendable, environmental or civic spirited reason. I am quite
satisfied that the sole purpose of the proceedings is to inflict damage upon its
competitor Lagan and I am satisfied that that is an improper purpose for the
commencement of proceedings and an improper use of the process of the Courts.”
(emphasis added)
45. In this case, the fact the Plaintiff confirmed that he had put his other proceedings on
hold till these proceedings were progressed, proves the point. If he were able to achieve his real
purpose through this case, he would have no need to progress those proceedings, which
challenge the powers of sale of the mortgage and the property. Thus, these proceedings have
an alternative and, in my view, an improper purpose and as such, on that ground alone I will
dismiss them.
46. However, his whole case is a made-up fiction, as anyone with a true knowledge of the
law would know. The fact the Plaintiff is a litigant in person is no excuse. It is clear he has
taken advice and, in my opinion, bad advice. I sincerely hope he has not paid for it, because if
I did, it was a waste of money. As O’Moore J. said in O’Hara referred to above: -
“It is a matter of some regret that the persons who are peddling this form of claim (to
litigants who do not have the benefit of professional representation) have not yet
themselves been made accountable for the pointless legal costs and expenses which
their activities have generated.”.

Conclusion and Final Order
47. Therefore, I find that any processing of the Plaintiff’s data, occurred in order to
complete the sale and was clearly incidental and necessary for complying with the obligations
of the Solicitors’ contract of retainer, the sale and their contractual and statutory obligations.
In those circumstances it was not illegal.
48. The Plaintiff's arguments are based on an allegation, as yet unproved, that the transfer
of the PTSB loan to Start was somehow illegal. That issue no doubt will be the subject of
further applications in the other set proceedings. However, for the purposes of this case, is clear
the court must accept the transfer on its face. To do otherwise would be to allow any litigant to
prevent any convenience, in any circumstances that was not to their liking. That would give
rise to an intolerable pressure on commercial life and the legal system.
49. The Solicitors had a legitimate reason for utilising the information. Any argument that
the purpose is related to money laundering is both abusive and nonsense. They are doing no
such thing.
50. The processing was necessary for the work of a solicitor working in the conveyance.
The balancing test flows from the necessity. There is no harm to the Plaintiff from these steps.
51. The Plaintiff's argument that somehow or other his rights under Article 5 and 6 of the
GDPR have been breached are misplaced at best and mischievous at worst. I am satisfied that
there is no real risk of injustice by striking out the proceedings and that they cannot be saved
by an amendment.
52. I am also satisfied that they are a collateral attack by the Plaintiff on the sale of the
property to the purchasers. That is clear from the proceedings themselves, but also the second
motion before the Court in which he has sought orders for disclosure and proprietary type

injunctions. It is an improper use of procedure. In my view, for all those reasons, the case is
bound to fail and accordingly I shall dismiss it.
53. As to legal costs, my provisional view is that the First and Second Named Defendants,
having been successful in these proceedings, would be entitled to recover their costs against
the Plaintiff in accordance with the default position under Section 169 of the Legal Services
Regulation Act 2015. However, if the Plaintiff wishes to argue for an alternative order, he may
apply within seven days to have the matter listed for a short hearing on costs. If, however, a
hearing on costs is requested and if, having heard the parties, the Court makes the order it has,
provisionally indicated, then the Plaintiff may be at risk of having to pay the additional costs
incurred as a result of the hearing.

title: SM and Anor -v- Dublin City Council
text:
THE HIGH COURT
JUDICIAL REVIEW
RECORD NUMBER 2024/1345 JR
[2026] IEHC 73
IN THE MATTER OF
THE HOUSING (MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS) ACT 2009
between
SM
and
KM
(A MINOR SUING BE HER MOTHER AND NEXT FRIEND)
APPLICANTS
and
DUBLIN CITY COUNCIL
RESPONDENT
Judgment of Mr Justice Cian Ferriter delivered 13 February 2026
Introduction
1. This judgment deals with the applicants’ application for the costs of these judicial
proceedings which both parties accept have become moot since the proceedings were
instituted in November 2024.
Background
2. The background to the matter is as follows.
1

3. On 11 November 2024, the applicants (a mother and minor daughter) were granted
leave to apply by way of judicial review for an order quashing the decision of the
respondent (“the Council”) of 2 August 2024 refusing to disregard the order of
priority given to a household under an allocation scheme where the household is
being provided with social housing support pursuant to section 22(7) of the Housing
(Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 2009 (“the 2009 Act”). In essence, the applicants were
seeking to quash a decision of the Council refusing to give them priority on the
Council’s housing list.
4. The applicants had been on the Council’s ordinary social housing list since June 2018.
The first named applicant (for ease in this judgment, “the applicant”) averred in her
grounding affidavit in the leave application to the fact that she and her daughter (the
second named applicant) had been subject to physical and psychological domestic
violence by her former partner and that her daughter had made significant allegations
of sexual abuse against that man. The applicant averred that she could not understand
why the Council considered that she remained safe in the house she and her daughter
were living in (which had formerly been owned with her ex partner) or why her
experiences of domestic violence were not sufficiently exceptional. She stated that she
simply wanted her daughter and herself to live free from danger.
5. The applicant had applied to the Council under section 22 of the 2009 Act for priority
on the social housing list in 2019 on the basis of Exceptional Social Grounds. That
application was refused. She applied for priority again in September 2023 when her
former partner’s behaviour continued to worsen. This was refused on 13 December
2023 and a review was then sought of that decision. The outcome of that review, dated
26 March 2024, was to refuse the review. On 3 April 2024, the applicant’s solicitors
requested that the review be revisited and on 17 April 2024 the Council maintained its
decision on review.
6. On 2 August 2024, the Council issued a further decision, being the decision
challenged in these proceedings, maintaining its refusal to place the applicant on the
priority list. The officer dealing with the application took the view that she was unable
to make a recommendation for priority. She referenced in her decision letter the fact
2

that the applicant had told a senior social worker who assessed her application in
September 2023 that she was not nervous about remaining close to where her ex
partner lived as she had an active protection order in place. (The applicant takes
fundamental issue with this version of events). The officer said she spoke to the gardai
and was informed that there were no incidents since 2020 of a breach of the safety
order and was of the view that the appropriate legal remedy of a safety order was
effective in the case. She referenced the fact that it was not uncommon for a family to
continue to reside in the home and an alleged perpetrator of domestic violence to
move out where there is a safety order in place and that therefore this circumstance
would not be considered exceptional or significantly more urgent than other
applicants under the exceptional social grounds scheme. Again, the applicant takes
issue with this characterisation of events.
7. The applicant’s solicitors sent further correspondence in September and October 2024
in essence inviting the Council to revisit its decision. On 22 October 2024, the
applicant’s solicitor sent a pre-action letter to the Council.
8. The applicant and her daughter were provided with short term emergency
accommodation by Sonas from 26 September 2024.
9. Following the institution of the proceedings by the grant of leave to apply for judicial
review on 11 November 2024, the Council wrote to the applicant’s solicitors on 9
December 2024. That letter re-iterated the basis of the Council's position that the
applicants were not entitled to priority on the housing list. The letter pointed out that
the applicants had been on the social housing list since June 2018 and were currently
no. 114 on that list. The letter stated that by reason of the ordinary operation of the list
the applicants were within the offer zone for “choice based lettings” not as a result of
exceptional circumstances but because of their length of time on the list. The letter
stated that in the expected course a property would become available early in 2025
within the applicant’s chosen area for which she would qualify.
10. The letter proposed an adjournment of the matter before the court on 10 December
2024 to facilitate engagement on the proposed choice based letting issue “with a view
to resolving the matter”.
3

11. This letter was replied to by the applicant’s solicitors on 18 December 2024 in which
it was noted that accommodation had been offered to the applicants for the purposes
of resolving the proceedings. This characterisation of the Council’s letter of 9
December 2024 was rejected in a letter from the Council of 8 January 2025. That
letter reiterated the Council's position that the applicant's request for housing was
being dealt with on the basis of her position on the ordinary social housing list and not
otherwise. The letter also appeared to contain additional reasons for refusing the
applicant’s application for priority which had not been contained within the decision
of 2 August 2024. Those additional reasons included the fact that the applicant had a
permanent job. The letter also stated that the Council was unaware of any evidence
that the applicant had experienced any domestic violence since 2019 when an
appropriate protection order was procured from the District Court. The applicant was
particularly exercised about this assertion in light of the material and evidence she had
previously put before the Council and before the court by way of grounding affidavit
in the proceedings.
12. On 11 March 2025 the applicants issued an application to amend their statement of
grounds to challenge the additional reasons set out in the letter of 8 January 2025. The
court granted permission to amend the statement of grounds on 25 March 2025.
13. The provision of the choice based letting was delayed by a number of months, the
Council says due to circumstances outside its control. This led to the proceedings
being adjourned some 11 times until the promised accommodation materialised.
14. The applicant was formally offered housing on 11 April 2025 which was accepted on
that date.
15. Both sides accept that the effect of the Council's offer of property pursuant to the
ordinary housing list and the applicant’s acceptance of same is to render the
proceedings moot.
Legal principles
4

16. The relevant legal principles in relation to the determination of costs in mootness
situations are not in dispute and are set out in the judgment of Murray J. in the Court
of Appeal in Hughes v Revenue Commissioners [2023] 3 IR 393 (“Hughes”). There is
a dispute as to the proper application of those principles to the facts.
17. As Murray J noted in Hughes the jurisprudence has evolved in recent years from a
position where the focus in resolving costs applications in mootness situations was on
the reasonableness of the applicant’s actions in instituting or maintaining the
proceedings up to the point of mootness, to a position where the focus was on the
cause of the mootness. (para 29)
18. The relevant principles are set out at paragraphs 31 to 33 of his judgment as follows:
“31. First, where the mootness arises as a result of an event that is entirely
independent of the actions of the parties to the proceedings, the fairest
outcome will generally be that the parties should bear the costs themselves.
Neither is responsible for the mootness, and neither should have to pay for
costs rendered unnecessary by an event for which they bear no responsibility.
32.Second, however, where the mootness arises because of the actions of one
of the parties alone and where those actions (a) can be said to follow from the
fact of the proceedings so that but for the proceedings they would not have
been undertaken, or (b) are properly characterised as ‘unilateral’ or –
perhaps – (c) are such that they could reasonably have been taken before the
proceedings, or before all of the costs ultimately incurred in the proceedings
were suffered, the costs should often be borne by the party whose actions have
resulted in the case becoming moot. In the first of these situations, it can be
fairly said that there was an event which costs can and should follow in
accordance with conventional principle. In the second, it will frequently be
proper that the party who is responsible for the unilateral action which results
in the mootness should bear the costs. In the third, it might be said that where
a party who could reasonably have acted so as to prevent the other party from
incurring costs failed to do so, it is proper that they should have to discharge
those costs.
5

33.The third general proposition addresses the particular position of statutory
bodies. Agencies with obligations in public law cannot be expected to suspend
the discharge of their statutory functions simply because there are extant legal
proceedings relating to the prior exercise of their powers. They must be free to
continue to exercise those powers in accordance with their legal obligations.
At the same time, it would be wrong if under the guise of exercising their
powers in the normal way, the statutory authority both effectively conceded an
extant claim, and avoided the legal costs that would otherwise attend such a
concession. The cases strike a balance between these two considerations by
suggesting that where the mootness arises because a statutory body makes a
new decision in the exercise of its legal powers, the court should look at the
circumstances giving rise to that new decision in order to decide whether it
constitutes a ‘unilateral act’ for these purposes. If the new decision is caused
by a change in the relevant circumstances occurring between the time of the
first decision, and of the second, the Court might not treat the new decision as
a ‘unilateral act’ and may accordingly make no order as to costs. If, however,
there has been no such change in circumstances so that the body has simply
changed its mind, costs may be awarded against it. If the respondent wishes to
contend that there has been a change in circumstances it is a matter for it to
place before the court sufficient evidence to allow the Court to assess whether
and if so to what extent it can fairly be said that this is so. This requires the
respondent to establish that there was a change in the underlying
circumstances sufficient to justify, in whole or in part, it being appropriate to
characterise the proceedings as having become moot by reason of a change in
external circumstances. In conducting this analysis, the Court should not
embark upon a determination of the merits of the underlying case.”
19. Murray J stresses, at para 34, that “each of these three propositions present a general
approach rather than a set of fixed, rigid rules. The starting point is that the court has
an overriding discretion in relation to the awarding of costs” and the propositions he
summarises from the case law are intended to guide the exercise of that discretion.
6

20. He helpfully suggested the following four questions in approaching the question of
where an appropriate costs order lay in mootness cases based on the principles he
summarised:
“ (i) What was the specific event that resulted in the action becoming moot?
(ii) Was that event the result of an occurrence outside the control of either
party?
(iii) Was that event caused by the unilateral actions of one of the parties?
(iv) If the answer to (iii) is in the affirmative, has the person responsible for the
mootness established that their actions were not undertaken in response to
the proceedings?”
Evidence and Submissions on the Costs question
21. Affidavits were sworn by both sides in respect of the costs question, which I shall
refer to where appropriate below, and I also received helpful written and oral
submissions from counsel on both sides.
Discussion
Applicant successful on “event”?
22. The applicant contends that she has been wholly successful in relation to the event
under section 169 of the Legal Services Regulation Act 2015 such that she should be
entitled to her full costs.
23. She says that the clear purpose of the proceedings was to secure housing from the
Council and that ultimately shortly after the institution of the proceedings she
succeeded in getting such housing. Whatever about the legal characterisation of the
basis of the route to housing ultimately offered to her by the Council she says as a
matter of fact she was successful on the core event of whether or not she should get
housing from the Council.
24. I am not persuaded by this submission. The event for the purposes of section 169 is
inevitably determined by the issues raised and relief sought in the proceedings. In her
7

statement of grounds, the applicant was clearly challenging the failure of the Council
to afford her priority status under section 22(7) of the 2009 Act. If the Council had
subsequent to the institution of the proceedings, and in response to the institution of
the proceedings, determined that it had changed its mind and she was entitled to be
put on the priority list, she might then be in a position to contend that she was
successful on the event (in accordance with the analysis of Murray J. set out at para
32 of Hughes quoted above).
25. However, the Council did not reconsider its decision as regards to the applicant being
put on the priority list and continued in its correspondence to stand over the validity
of its decision in that regard.
26. I do not think that I can hold that the applicant was successful on the event for the
purposes of section 169 in the circumstances.
27. Rather, it seems to me that I must proceed to apply the principles established in the
case law on costs in mootness cases as summarised in Hughes.
Mootness caused by Council’s unilateral actions?
28. The applicant contends that, as an alternative to her section 169 arguments, the
mootness was caused by a unilateral act of the Council in offering her housing. She
further contends that a consideration of the circumstances as a whole both prior to the
institution of the proceedings and during the currency of the proceedings, makes clear
that the offer of housing was not in the ordinary course of business but that the
Council instead timed its offer of housing so as to render the proceedings moot.
29. I accept that it can reasonably be said that the proceedings became moot as a result of
the Council's actions in offering the applicant a house by virtue of her place near the
top of the ordinary waiting list. The real question that arises is whether on the
evidence before me the Council has discharged the onus of demonstrating that (in the
words of Murray J in Hughes at para 39(iv)) the actions of the Council which resulted
in the applicant being offered housing were not undertaken in response to the
proceedings.
8

30. In this regard, an affidavit was sworn by Mary Hayes a senior official in the Council
who is a director of the Dublin regional homeless executive of the Council. In that
affidavit she averred that in its letter of 9 December 2024 the Council noted that the
applicants had been on the social housing list since 27 June 2018 and their current
position at that time was no. 114. The Council advised that by ordinary operation of
the housing list the applicants were within the offer zone for choice based lettings not
as a result of their exceptional circumstances but because of the length of time they
had been on the social housing list. The Council advised that it was expected that her
property would become available in the first quarter 2025 within the applicants’
chosen area.
31. Ms. Hayes in her affidavit at para 22 (referencing the Council’s letter of 8 January
2025) averred that the property had not been offered for the purposes of resolving the
proceedings but rather that it had become available in the ordinary course of the
Council's housing management and was offered to the applicant reflective of her place
in the social housing list and not otherwise. She averred that following the applicants’
application for a property based on the choice based lettings advertisements, the
applicants were successful in their application based on their position on the social
housing list and entered into a tenancy agreement on 11 April 2025. Ms Hayes avers
that the application had become moot not because of any change of policy or unusual
act of the Council but as a direct result of the Council carrying out its statutory
functions in accordance with its published policies.
32. I do not believe that I can look behind those averments. This was not a case of the
Council accepting that the applicants were entitled to priority on the list, contrary to
the Council's previous decisions, or that the applicants were otherwise “bumped up”
the list in order to effectively dispose of the proceedings.
33. It seems to me in the circumstances that the Council had discharged the onus of
showing that housing was not offered to the applicants as a response to these
proceedings.
9

34. On that basis, it seems to me that the default rule in mootness cases that each party
bear its own costs should prevail subject to considering whether general interests of
justice factors require that the applicants should nonetheless get some or all of their
costs, to which I next turn.
Interests of Justice nonetheless merit a costs order in favour of applicants?
35. Counsel for the applicants submitted that, even if I were not with the applicants on the
issues addressed above, there were particular exceptional factors on the facts of this
case which would render it just for the court to award the applicants their costs or at
least some of their costs.
36. Firstly, she relied on the fact that following the impugned decision of 2 August 2024,
her solicitor wrote a number of letters to the Council in September and October 2024,
inviting the Council to reconsider its position and threatening litigation. She says that
she did not get a substantive response to these proceedings and it was reasonable to
issue the proceedings in the circumstances.
37. Secondly, and relatedly, she pointed to the failure on the part of the Council to make
clear prior to the institution of the proceedings that the applicant was nearing a point
where she would be offered housing in any event. This was a matter within the
Council’s knowledge and could not have been within hers. This important information
was only made clear by the Council in its letter of 9 December 2024 after the
institution of the proceedings; if the applicant has been told about an imminent offer
of accommodation, she would not have needed to issue the proceedings in the first
place. (In para 18 of her third affidavit the applicant averred that “it was only in the
Council's correspondence of 9 December 2024 that it was stated to me and my
solicitors for the first time that the Council anticipated that I would be accommodated
within a definite timeframe. Had I been aware of this and known at any stage prior to
the institution of these proceedings that we might be accommodated shortly that
would obviously have influenced my decision as to whether or not to bring these
proceedings. I do not understand why I was never told this.”)
10

38. Thirdly, she pointed to the fact that after the Council indicated in its letter of 9
December 2024 that the applicant could now be offered ordinary housing in early
2025, that there were some 11 adjournments of the proceedings between January and
June 2025 to facilitate the Council in fact being able to deliver such housing, which
was in essence a period of time where the applicants acted very reasonably but which
the council used to align its ordinary course of business case (i.e. the delivery of
accommodation via the ordinary list) with rendering the proceedings moot.
39. Fourthly, she says that notwithstanding its position that the proceedings were not
necessary particularly once an offer of housing had been made, the Council
nonetheless sought to supplement the reasons for its original refusal of priority by its
letter of 8 January 2025, including the tendentious reason from the applicant’s
perspective that the Council was unaware of any evidence that the applicant had
experienced any domestic violence since 2019, a contention which the applicant
found particularly distressing in light of the material which she had put before both
the Council and the Court. This led to the applicants applying without opposition to
amend their statement of grounds to cover these new reasons which the applicant
maintained was further evidence of the unlawfulness of the Council's position on her
application for priority status on the list.
40. In response to the submission that the “flurry of activity” that led to an offer of
housing was a direct response to the issue of the proceedings, and that the Council
could and should have communicated the fact that an offer of housing in the ordinary
course was imminent before the proceedings issued, counsel for the Council indicated
that the Council could not lawfully have made representations as to the potential
availability of a property prior to the institution of proceedings in circumstances
where the applicants’ entitlements to be offered a property by virtue of their place on
the waiting lists had not yet arisen.
41. As is clear from the case law the court cannot embark upon a determination of the
merits of the underlying case in conducting an analysis of the appropriate costs order
in a mootness case.
11

42. It seems to me that both parties acted sensibly in ensuring that the proceedings were
adjourned once the offer of housing was made in the ordinary course. I do not believe
that I can either reward the applicants or penalise the Council for the circumstances
that led to the applicants successfully applying to amend their proceedings following
the Council's letter of 8 January 2024, as to do so would be to stray into the merits of
the underlying claims in the proceedings.
43. While I am sympathetic to the view that the institution of the proceedings could have
been avoided by the Council taking the step in response to the applicant’s pre-action
letter of 22 October 2024 of making internal enquiries as to where the applicant stood
on the ordinary social housing list and whether this was likely to result in the offer of
accommodation in the ordinary course within a matter of weeks or months, I note that
the Council’s position in correspondence (in its letter of 14 February 2025) was that
that the applicant could have established her position on the list via the relevant portal
on the Council’s website and I am mindful of the pressures on the Council as regards
accommodation applications and the fact that the focus of the applicant’s complaint
was on the failure to put her on the priority list. I also think there is substance in the
submission that the Council could not have made representations as to her likely
entitlement to accommodation until her place on the list in fact ripened into such an
entitlement. In the circumstances, I do not think that the Council’s conduct in this
regard is such as to merit an award of costs against it.
44. I can understand the first applicant’s upset at being faced with a position in
correspondence (and in written submissions) on the costs application to the effect that
it was not accepted that there was evidence that she had suffered domestic violence in
recent years. However, the 2 August 2024 decision is more nuanced on the applicant’s
circumstances and references comments said to have been made by the applicant
herself (which she denies) and inquiries made of Gardai. In the absence of
determining the merits of the applicant’s case and the Council's position on that case
as reflected in its correspondence, which I cannot do, I do not believe I can hold that
the Council’s stance on this issue was so unreasonable as to entitle the applicant to all
or some of her costs of the proceedings.
12

45. The applicant I should stress seems to me to have acted reasonably in all the
circumstances both before and during the proceedings given the desperate situation
she regarded herself and her daughter as having been in. Her legal team acted
tirelessly, skilfully and appropriately on her behalf. Nothing in this judgment should
be taken to detract from that.
46. However, having accepted as I do the bona fides of the position set out on affidavit by
Ms Hayes on behalf of the Council, and for the additional reasons set out above, I do
not think I can safely hold that the interests of justice merit a costs order against the
Council.
Conclusion
47. In the circumstances, it seems to me that the appropriate costs order is to make no
orders as to costs.
48. My provisional view is that the same order should also apply to the costs hearing. I
will hear the parties should they wish to apply for any order to the contrary.
13

title: Conway -v- Director Of Public Prosecutions
text:
[2026] IEHC 76
RECORD NUMBER 2024/157 JR
THE HIGH COURT
BETWEEN
CONWAY
APPLICANT
AND
DIRECTOR OF PUBLIC PROSECUTIONS
RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT of Mr. Justice Michael Twomey delivered on the 12th day of February, 2026
INTRODUCTION
1. When an application is made to set aside a judgment of a court because it is claimed that it
was not clear or transparent enough, it is important to bear in mind that there is a difference
between judgments of the District and Circuit Court and those of the High Court, the Court
of Appeal and the Supreme Court.
1

2. In the District and Circuit Courts, the judgment is usually delivered orally (ex tempore),
due to the large caseload in those courts, while in the other courts, the judgment is often
delivered in writing which allows time for careful reflection on the language to be used
(sometimes over several drafts), and in the case of the appellate courts, with input from
judicial colleagues.
3. Thus, when criticising a judgment of a District or Circuit Court for its lack of clarity or lack
of transparency, it is important to bear in mind the difference in the time afforded to a
District or Circuit Court judge to formulate a judgment, compared to judgments of the other
courts, as well as the practical realities regarding the context in which judgments in the
District or Circuit Court are given.
4. This is important background for this case, as it concerns a claim by the applicant (“Mr.
Conway”) that there is an absence of transparency and clarity in Crowe J.’s judgment. In
that judgment, she refused Mr. Conway’s application to have charges against him, for the
use and supply of cocaine, struck out).
5. It is to be noted that, for the purposes of this application, the words spoken by the trial judge
have been reduced to writing, since there is a transcript of the hearing. However, it remains
an oral judgment and so for the reasons stated, it is very different in format from a written
judgment issued by the High Court/Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court.
6. As a general point, it seems to this Court, that this difference between judgments in the
Circuit Court (or District Court) and the other courts, needs to be borne in mind, when those
judgments are criticised for not having clarified a point which might, with the benefit of
hindsight, have been clarified, or if they failed to make explicit something, that might be
regarded as implicit.
2

BACKGROUND
7. This is an application which was heard by this Court on 23 January 2025, to quash, by way
of judicial review, an order of the Circuit Court.
8. At that hearing in the Circuit Court, Mr. Conway sought to have charges on indictment
which had been brought against him dismissed, because of the alleged insufficiency of the
evidence. The application to dismiss Mr. Conway’s indictment was brought under s.4E of
the Criminal Procedure Act, 1967 (the “1967 Act”). The charges he sought to have
dismissed related to the possession, and possession for the purposes of supply, of cocaine
(under the Misuse of Drugs Acts 1977 and 1984).
9. On 8 November 2023, the Circuit Court gave its judgment refusing to dismiss the charges
against Mr. Conway.
10. Mr. Conway’s position is that the trial judge was incorrect in law, when he says she relied
on the Notice of Additional Evidence (the “Additional Evidence”) which was made in the
case, when she reached her decision to refuse his application to dismiss the charges.
11. The background to this issue is that on 24 July 2023 Mr. Conway’s application to dismiss
was heard and one of the grounds was that the Certificate of Analysis from Forensic Science
Ireland was flawed.
12. It was claimed by counsel for Mr. Conway that that there was no reference in that
certificate to handling, transmission and storage. At this hearing, counsel for the DPP
purported to call a witness at that hearing, to which Mr. Conway’s counsel objected, and
the hearing was adjourned to allow the DPP to take instructions and make legal submissions
on the calling of the witness.
13. Then on 26 July 2023, the DPP served the Additional Evidence on Mr. Conway in relation
to the Certificate of Analysis from Forensic Science Ireland, which it was claimed by Mr.
3

Conway was intended to rectify the flaws that he had highlighted at the hearing on 24 July
2023.
14. When the hearing resumed on 27 July 2023, the DPP accepted that it could not call a witness
at a s. 4E hearing (unless the interests of justice required it). This dealt with the witness
issue which had led to the adjournment in the first place. However, at this hearing, counsel
for Mr. Conway also objected to the Court receiving the Additional Evidence as the s. 4E
hearing had already commenced. He submitted that the court had to be satisfied that it was
in the interests of justice for additional evidence to be received under s.4E(6) of the 1967
Act. However, in reliance on DPP v Tuite [1983] WJSC-CCA 2336, the DPP submitted
that the DPP had a general power to serve Notices of Additional Evidence and that this
right extended to s.4E of the 1967 Act.
15. At this juncture, it is important to note that by the time of the hearing before this Court, it
became clear that the DPP does not now claim that there is a general power to serve Notices
of Additional Evidence. Instead, at the hearing before this Court, the DPP’s position had
changed, as the DPP agreed with Mr. Conway’s submission that the court must be satisfied
that receiving any additional evidence is in the interests of justice, before it will be
admitted.)
16. In any event, after hearing what were then the conflicting positions taken by Mr. Conway
and the DPP, submissions, the trial judge adjourned the matter for judgement, which was
given on 8 November 2023.
The wording of the judgment which is the subject of challenge
17. When giving her judgment on 8 November 2023, the trial judge accepted the DPP’s
submission, in reliance on DPP v Tuite, that the State had a general power (and so without
being satisfied that it is in the interests of justice) to serve Notices of Additional Evidence
4

during a s. 4E application (albeit that the DPP now accepts that its submission on the state
of the law was incorrect).
18. In its written submissions to this Court, the DPP put the matter as follows:
‘It is accepted that only such proposed evidence as is served on an accused prior to the
commencement of the [s. 4E] application is admissible on the issue. Any proposed
evidence served after the commencement of the [s 4.E] application can only be
considered if the judge is “satisfied that it is in the interests of justice”. As a result,
only the proposed evidence contained in the Book [of Evidence] and [the Notice of
Additional Evidence] served on the 23rd July 2023 was available to be considered,
absent a determination that it was in the interests of justice that the proposed evidence
contained in the [Notice of Additional Evidence] dated the 26th July 2023 could be
considered.’
19. However, because the trial judge accepted the DPP’s previous submissions (that there was
a general right to consider additional evidence), Mr. Conway claims that the trial judge’s
decision, not to dismiss the charges against him arises from an error of law and was
therefore made without jurisdiction and so is void and of no legal effect.
20. He therefore seeks an order from this Court quashing the trial judge’s refusal to dismiss the
charges against him.
21. The DPP accepts that if the trial judge had relied on the Additional Evidence, in reaching
her decision not to dismiss, then the DPP would not be resisting this judicial review.
22. However, the DPP states that while the trial judge did refer to the Additional Evidence, it
is clear from her judgment that she did not rely on that evidence in making her decision.
For this reason, the DPP says that there was no error of law affecting that decision to dismiss
Mr. Conway’s application, and the trial judge’s decision was made within jurisdiction and
is therefore valid.
5

Did the trial judge rely on the Additional Evidence to dismiss the application?
23. It is clear that the key issue in this judicial review is whether the trial judge did, or did not,
rely on the Additional Evidence to reach her decision to refuse to dismiss the charges. To
determine this issue, it is necessary to set out the relevant paragraphs from the trial judge’s
judgement of 8 November 2023:
‘The chain of custody and the exhibits and the certificate of analysis: it was asserted at
the first hearing that the chain of custody was insufficient in the intervening period
between the first hearing on the 24th of July and the 27th of July. Notices of additional
evidence were served which purport to deal with these matters. Issue was then taken by
the defence with the service of additional evidence. It is of course the case that the
prosecution has a wide latitude in respect of the service of additional evidence right up
to and including, less ideally, sometimes during hte running of a trial, and the case of
DPP v Gerard Anthony Tuite was opened to me and is authority for that proposition
stretching back some time. In relation to the service of additional evidence, the serving
of a notice pursuant to section 4(e) does not act as a bar on the prosecution adducing
additional evidence in the view of this Court. Indeed, it is often done right up to the
start of the trial or even during trial, and I accept that proposition as proffered by the
prosecution.’
24. In relation to this paragraph, the DPP submitted to this Court that:
‘It is certainly clear that, in that little paragraph, the trial judge does come to the
conclusion that she is entitled to rely on additional evidence served after the
commencement of the s.4(E) hearing, and as I have already conceded I don’t think that
is correct in law and if that was the end of the matter, none of us would be here today
because the Director wouldn’t be seeking to stand over the decision. But that has to be
read in conjunction with the next paragraph [...].’
6

25. The next paragraph of the trial judge’s judgment was then duly opened to this Court by
counsel for the DPP and this reads as follows:
‘Arguments raised in respect of the chain of evidence and the certificate of analysis
should they be relevant, are matters in my view which should be dealt with at the trial
and in that regard I note the decision of the Court of Appeal in Cruise v O’Donnell in
[2008]. It is notable that the determination of disputed issues of law and fact remains
the preserve of the trial judge, and they are most appropriate to be dealt with by a trial
court on an application for dismissal. The Court confines itself to the evidence
presented in the book of evidence taking the prosecution case at its strongest on the
assumption that the evidence disclosed is admissible, and that is what this Court has to
do in this particular case. Here there are plainly contested issues of fact requiring
resolution, but which are in the view of this Court not suitable for disposal other than
by the trial court and therefore, I am refusing the application in relation to the matters
that have been outlined.’ (emphasis added)
26. Before interpreting these two paragraphs, it is important to have regard to the contextual
background to the references to the ‘book of evidence’ and to ‘additional evidence’ in both
paragraphs. In this regard, this hearing (at which judgment was delivered) on the 8
November 2023, was the third and final hearing in relation to the application to dismiss. It
is relevant to note that, at the earlier hearing on 24 July 2023, counsel for the DPP purported
to call a guard to give evidence and counsel for Mr. Conway made specific reference to
these two categories of evidence (the book of evidence and the Additional Evidence), when
he objected in the following terms:
‘Counsel for the DPP: [...] In respect of the arrest, I would call Garda Duhova to
give evidence about the arrest.
7

Counsel for Mr. Conway: Judge, I would object to that in circumstances where this
application is grounded on foot of what is contained in the book of evidence. So, I don’t
have any capacity to serve further evidence unless it’s been served by way of notice of
additional evidence, which would be the appropriate way in anticipation of these
applications, which was done in relation to Garda Byrne. That’s why obviously we can
proceed with this matter in the absence of Garda Walsh who’s the prosecuting member.
I don’t think it’s appropriate to call witnesses to add further evidence that isn’t
contained in the book or contained in the notice of additional evidence. (emphasis
added)
27. Indeed, there are numerous other references to these two distinct categories of evidence.
For example, at the hearing on 24 July 2023 counsel for Mr. Conway made the following
statements:
‘There’s three aspects that I want to object to in relation to the book of evidence and
also additional evidence’1
‘if I could bring the Court to page 4 of the book of evidence’2
‘if I could bring the Court’ attention to page 26 of the book of evidence?’3
‘The Court will see from the list of exhibits contained at page 28 of the book of
evidence’4
‘I don’t know if the Court has a copy of the notice of additional evidence’5
‘That’s at page 19 of the book of evidence’6
1 Transcript of 24 July 2023, pg. 1.
2 ibid, pg. 1.
3 ibid, pg. 2
4 ibid, pg. 2.
5 ibid, pg. 3.
6 ibid, pg. 3.
8

‘I would object to that in circumstances where this application is grounded on foot of
what is contained in the book of evidence’.7
28. Also, at the hearing on the 27 July 2023, there were further references to these two
categories of evidence when counsel for Mr. Conway submitted:
‘Yes, but I’m just saying that’s not contained anywhere within the papers’ (here,
apparently referring to both categories together) 8
‘my friend is referring to a notice of additional evidence that was served yesterday’9
‘we were served a notice of additional evidence yesterday’10
29. All of this is important background to the trial judge’s decision on the 8 November 2023,
because the trial judge also separately refers, in the above extract of her judgment, to the
‘book of evidence’ and the ‘additional evidence’. For this reason, it seems clear to this Court
that the trial judge’s decision was based on there being a very clear difference between the
book of evidence and additional evidence.
30. With this clear distinction between these two categories of evidence as the background to
her judgment, it is crucial to note that she goes on to state that she ‘confines’ herself to the
evidence presented in the book of evidence, in reaching her decision.
31. It seems to this Court that the purpose of her ‘confining’ herself to the book of evidence in
the second paragraph of the above extract was because in the first paragraph she had
concluded that she could, if she wanted to, rely on the Additional Evidence.
32. However, this conclusion (that she could rely on Additional Evidence) does not affect the
legality of her decision. This is because in the first paragraph she says that she could rely
on the Additional Evidence, but in the very next paragraph, she expressly states that she is
7 ibid, pg. 8.
8 Transcript 27 July 2023, pg. 3.
9 ibid, pg. 4.
10 ibid, pg. 4.
9

‘confining’ herself to the book of evidence. To put it another way, the logical conclusion
of confining herself to the book of evidence must be that she was not relying on the
Additional Evidence. After all, if the trial judge was relying on the Additional Evidence, as
claimed by Mr. Conway, there would have been no need to say that she was ‘confining’
herself to the book of evidence.
Should the judgment be set aside as it is not clear or transparent?
33. Mr. Conway also claims that even if the DPP is correct that the trial judge nonetheless
chose not to rely on the Additional Evidence, the trial judge should have explicitly stated
this fact. On this basis, he made oral submissions that there was an absence of clarity and
transparency on the judge’s part and so claims that this is a basis for setting aside her
decision. In his written submissions, he states:
‘if the trial judge reached her conclusions based solely on [the book of evidence] she
would have said so and a fortiori where, in her judgement, she expressly held that [the
DPP] was entitled to adduce further evidence, by way of a notice of additional evidence
[..]’
34. In relation to this argument, the DPP rejects that there is any illegality in the trial judge’s
decision such that it should be set aside, but the DPP accepts that it might have been
preferrable if the trial judge had explicitly stated that she was not relying on the Additional
Evidence.
35. As already noted, because of the volume of cases in that court, judgments in the Circuit
Court are usually given orally, without the time to clarify and improve judgments, which
judges in the High Court, Court of Appeal and Supreme Court, usually have. As a result,
oral judgments in the Circuit Court (and the District Court) will, with the benefit of
hindsight, always be capable of being further clarified or made more transparent. However,
10

those judgments cannot, in this Court’s view, be overly criticised for a failure to reference
submissions or otherwise clarify or make explicit matters, which are implicit.
36. In this instance, this Court does not accept that the trial judge’s decision should be set aside
because of an absence of transparency or an absence of clarification, which with the benefit
of hindsight, could have been added. The trial judge stated that she confined herself to the
book of evidence and it is a counsel of perfection to say that she should also have stated
that she was not relying on the Additional Evidence. It seems to this Court that Mr. Conway
could not have been under any misunderstanding as to the basis upon which the decision
was reached and this Court does not believe that oral judgments in busy Circuit Courts (or
District Courts) should be subject to 20:20 hindsight regarding terms that could or should
have been used.
CONCLUSION
37. For the reasons set out above, this Court finds that:
• the trial judge did not rely on the Additional Evidence when refusing to dismiss the
charges against Mr. Conway;
• any incorrect statement of the law by the trial judge (in reliance on the DPP’s
submissions) regarding the admissibility of evidence, did not affect the decision of
the trial judge; and
• the oral judgment was sufficiently clear and transparent, given the context in which
it was delivered.
Accordingly, this Court rejects the application to quash the decision of the trial judge.
38. This matter will be put in for mention a week from the date of delivery at 10.45 a.m. to deal
with any final orders and costs. However, on the assumption that it should not be necessary
to expend costs on a further court sitting, and in order to facilitate the parties agreeing all
11

outstanding matters, the parties have liberty to notify the Registrar if such a listing proves
to be unnecessary. This is particularly so in light of the clear implication from Word Perfect
Translation Services Ltd v Minister for Public Expenditure and Reform [2023] IECA 189
at para. 94, that there is an onus on lawyers to take a broad-brush approach to costs and not
to engage in the inefficient use of court resources and costly ‘nit-picking’.
12

title: I. M. -v- The International Protection Appeals Tribunal and Anor
text:
THE HIGH COURT
JUDICIAL REVIEW
Record No. 2024/1412 JR
[2026] IEHC 64
Between:
I. M.
Applicant
-and-
