THE INTERNATIONAL PROTECTION APPEALS TRIBUNAL
and THE MINISTER FOR JUSTICE
Respondents
Judgment of Mr Justice Cian Ferriter delivered on 11 February 2026
Introduction
1. In these judicial review proceedings, the applicant seeks to have quashed the decision
of the first named respondent ("the Tribunal") of 18 October 2024 in which it affirmed a
recommendation of the International Protection Office ("IPO") that the applicant be given
neither a refugee declaration nor a subsidiary protection declaration.
Background
2. The background to the matter is as follows. The applicant is a national of Georgia, born
on 19 October 2001. After his arrival in Ireland in April 2022, he made a claim for protection
to the Minister for Justice (“the Minister”) on the basis that he ran the risk of suffering
persecution in Georgia as a Russian speaker and had been discriminated against as such.
3. The applicant completed a questionnaire for the IPO. He was interviewed pursuant to
section 35 of the International Protection Act, 2015 (“the 2015 Act”) and a memorandum of
that interview was recorded and signed by him (“the s.35 interview”).

4. The applicant was informed by letter dated 1 March 2024 that the IPO recommended
he should not be given a refugee declaration nor a subsidiary protection declaration. The IPO
found that the Appellant had failed to establish a well-founded fear of persecution or a real risk
of serious harm in Georgia.
5. By Notice of Appeal dated 8 August 2024 the applicant appealed the recommendation
of the IPO to the Tribunal. In his notice of appeal, the applicant sought an oral hearing of his
appeal, thus requiring the Tribunal to make a decision in accordance with section 43(b) of the
2015 Act as to whether it was not in the interests of justice to have a papers-only appeal but
rather provide an oral hearing on his appeal (Georgia being deemed a safe country of origin
and therefore the statutory default position being that his appeal would be on the papers only)
and that this decision be notified to the applicant in advance of any decision on the appeal.
6. In a decision dated 18 October 2024 (“the decision”), which was communicated by
letter of that date, the applicant was notified that pursuant to section 46 of the 2015 Act the
Tribunal affirmed the recommendation of the IPO that he should be given neither a refugee
declaration nor a subsidiary protection declaration. In the decision, the Tribunal also ruled that
it was satisfied that the applicant was not entitled to an oral hearing of his appeal.
7. In order to set the issues arising on this judicial review in their appropriate context it is
necessary, before coming to the terms of the Tribunal’s decision, to summarise the terms of the
IPO’s recommendation and the grounds of the applicant’s appeal against that decision.
IPO Recommendation
8. The IPO officer, in her recommendation report dated 23 January 2024, found that the
applicant's account of his alleged discrimination as a Russian speaker in Georgia lacked detail,
was vague and contained inconsistencies, all of which undermined the credibility of his
claim. In his evidence, he had only given detail of one unpleasant experience, at a café in his
home town, arising from him being a Russian speaker. He said that this incident was the last
straw for him and made him decide to leave. The IPO officer noted that when it was put to
him that he had not referred to this incident in his questionnaire, he said he did not include it
because there were so many cases and he just forgot about them. He claimed the incident in the
2

café occurred in mid-April 2022, notwithstanding that he had left Georgia on 25 March
2022. It was put to him that Russian is a very common language in Georgia. His answer when
put to him that Russian is a very common language in Georgia was held by the IPO officer not
to have addressed the question put to him. The IPO officer stated that "The lack of detail,
vague responses and inconsistent account regarding his claim is deemed to further undermine
the credibility on this aspect of his claim". The IPO officer found that his answer, when given
an opportunity to say anything further about his adverse experiences, was that "there were a lot
of instances" which was a vague and non-specific answer.
9. His explanation for not reporting matters to the police was not accepted by reference to
COI information. The IPO officer held that his account in relation to his passport was
insufficiently detailed, contradictory and externally inconsistent; he initially said in his s. 35
interview that he had never had a passport, and then he said that he had a passport when he
arrived in London from Georgia, but threw away his passport in London as he approached the
officer to apply for international protection. The IPO officer concluded that she did not accept
on the balance of probabilities that the applicant was discriminated against in Georgia due to
his Russian heritage.
Appeal
10. On 15 March 2024 the applicant lodged a notice of appeal against the IPO
recommendation. In his appeal notice he requested an oral hearing of his appeal. In the grounds
of appeal releasing to this request, he contended that “the issues raised by the IPO can only
appropriately be dealt with by way of oral evidence and submissions due to the nature of the
reasons for refusal. No prejudice to the tribunal can be envisaged by the denial of an oral
hearing to our client, yet the potential of prejudice to the applicant is clear. The denial of an
oral hearing is not compatible with the principles of equivalence and effectiveness.”
11. The grounds of appeal denied that the applicant was inconsistent, vague and/or non-
specific about his claims. He said that his verbal testimony was the primary evidence which
was another reason why an oral hearing was required. He advanced various arguments as to
why his account should be accepted including the fact that “the applicant did not parrot an
account out by rote is suggestive of a real life lived experience and not of a narrative which has
3

been carefully rehearsed.” He contended that the IPO had failed to give him the benefit of the
doubt in circumstances where his account on the whole was coherent and plausible.
12. He also contended that Georgia was not in fact a safe country of origin.
Tribunal’s decision
13. I will shortly address in more detail the precise terms of the Tribunal's decision under
the relevant heads of challenge in these proceedings. However, for present purposes, I should
note that the Tribunal considered the applicant’s application for an oral hearing at some length
over the course of some 12 pages and concluded that it was not in the interests of justice to
provide an oral hearing. The Tribunal concluded that in the absence of any issue or new element
of the claim or specific detail that required to be probed, the applicant was in essence merely
seeking to repeat his evidence in an oral hearing before the tribunal. The Tribunal did not
consider that a mere restatement of his claim before the tribunal justified an oral hearing given
that no demeanour type findings had been made against him (at para 1.24).
14. The Tribunal then went on to assess the substance of his claim that as a Russian speaker
in Georgia people would consider him to support Russia and therefore submit him to
discrimination. The Tribunal considered that if he was genuinely in fear of persecution in
Georgia, he would have persisted with his application for international protection in the UK
and would not have been so vague about whether he had actually applied or not (para 4.2). The
Tribunal found that the wilful destruction of his passport seriously undermined the applicant's
credibility and evidenced a willingness to deceive. It said he was able to procure a photograph
of the passport for his labour market access requests which suggested a foreknowledge of its
potential use which was at odds with the otherwise vague account of his travel. The Tribunal
found that “this case has all the hallmarks of someone who has concealed what actually
happened to his passport and who was attempting to circumvent the immigration laws and/or
forum shop in respect of his claim for international protection rather than being someone who
has acted in the manner that would reasonably be expected from someone who was genuinely
afraid of persecution.” (para 4.2).
15. The Tribunal noted that there were no supporting documents in relation to the material
elements of the applicant’s claim noting he hadn't provided documents about his time living in
4

Russia nor did he provide his original passport. It concluded that this was not a claim where
the absence of documentation was understandable or reasonable (para 4.4).
16. The Tribunal found that his response to the IPO suggestion that there was no COI to
support his claim and that Russian is commonly spoken in Georgia was “vague”. The Tribunal
stated that it had reviewed the COI on file and could not see any obvious support for the
proposition that Georgian nationals who speak Russian as their primary language are
discriminated against. The Tribunal addressed a newspaper article from the Moscow Times
submitted on behalf of the applicant which suggested that Russian citizens in a cafe were asked
to confirm that they did not support Putin before being served and also stated that some people
were asked not to speak Russian or discuss politics when drunk and that some nightclubs or
other services refused service to Russian citizens or banks who requested that they confirmed
their position before doing business with them (para 4.7).
17. The Tribunal noted that the applicant was not a Russian citizen but a Georgian citizen
who speaks Russian. The Tribunal noted that there were news articles about ethnic Russians
facing discrimination but that the Tribunal was unable to locate anything in relation to ethnic
Georgians who speak Russian being discriminated against. It therefore concluded on balance
that while there was COI to suggest discrimination against Russian citizens and nationals in
Georgia there was no COI to support the applicant’s claim that he was discriminated against as
an ethnic Georgian who spoke Russian. (para 4.8).
18. The Tribunal further held on the question of the alleged discrimination against him as
a Russian speaker in Georgia (at para 4.9):
“[4.9] The Appellant’s account of the discrimination is quite vague and non specific.
He fears that he would be booed and would have a feeling of alienation. He claims that
he was sitting at a table and people next to him said that he should not use Russian in
public. He claims that he explained to them that he was ethnically Georgian but they
wouldn’t believe him so security had to intervene, which is vague, superficial and
generic account and, as set out above, does not derive support from the C.O.I. The
specific incident in the café is also vague and he claims it happened in mid April i.e.
after he had already left Georgia, according to his Questionnaire. When asked about
this he stated that he got confused and must look at a calendar.”
5

19. The Tribunal concluded as follows (at para 4.10):
“[4.10] Overall, the Tribunal considers that the Appellant has limited supporting
documentation. He has sought to tie his claim into the discrimination faced by ethnic
Russian people. His claim is vague, superficial and non specific. He gave contradictory
information at times. He made significant attempts to conceal information about his
passport, and if he destroyed or threw away same after he had arrived in the U.K., and
if he didn’t stick with his claim for International Protection there, this significantly
undermines his general credibility. Overall, the Tribunal considers that the negative
credibility indicators far outweigh the more limited positives in this case. On the
balance of probabilities the only material element of his claim which is accepted in his
Georgian nationality. There are no areas of genuine doubt or uncertainty, and he has
not established his general credibility, so the benefit of the doubt does not apply in this
particular case.”
Summary of parties’ positions
20. The applicant's case, in summary, is that the Tribunal fundamentally erred in failing to
give him an oral hearing on his appeal. The applicant further contends that various aspects of
the Tribunal's findings ought to have been put to him in advance as a matter of fair procedures
to give him an oral opportunity to comment (albeit that this group of grounds was not pressed
at the hearing before me). The applicant also submitted that the Tribunal had made a serious
error in mischaracterising the basis of his claim for international protection. The applicant also
submitted that the Tribunal had erred in the reliance it placed on his alleged failure to provide
documentation in support of his claims and had erred in its application of s. 28(7) of the 2015
Act.
21. The respondents, for their part, contended that there had been no breach of fair
procedures, that the Tribunal perfectly lawfully held that there was no entitlement to an oral
hearing on the facts of the case, that there had been no breach of s. 28(7), and no material error
in the characterisation of the applicant's claims. They also submitted that there had been no
error in relation to the view taken by the Tribunal on the absence of supporting documentation.
6

Consideration of issues arising
22. Logically, it makes sense to first address the applicant's contention that the Tribunal
erred in its decision in failing to direct an oral hearing on the facts of this case as, if the applicant
is correct in this contention, the Tribunal’s decision would necessarily have to be quashed and
the matter remitted for a fresh appeal hearing.
Error by Tribunal in refusing to provide an oral hearing on the appeal?
Decision not to provide oral hearing – separate decision required?
23. While the applicant obtained leave to argue the case that his right to fair procedures in
the Tribunal process was breached by a failure on the part of the Tribunal to communicate a
decision in advance of its ultimate decision, as to whether or not to hold an oral hearing, this
point was not pursued at the hearing before me. I think this was a sensible course for counsel
for the applicant to adopt in circumstances where a number of authorities (including my own
judgment in FP v IPAT [2022] IEHC 535 and the judgment of Hyland J in CC v IPAT [2023]
IEHC 636) make clear that there is no legal entitlement to a separate decision from the Tribunal
in respect of whether or not an oral hearing should be held pursuant to s. 43(b) of the 2015 Act.
24. I will turn accordingly to the applicant’s case that the Tribunal erred in not providing
him with an oral appeal hearing.
Tribunal erred in failing to provide an oral appeal hearing?
25. In my very recent judgment in KS v IPAT [2026] IEHC 13 (“KS”), I sought to
comprehensively survey the authorities in relation to the circumstances in which fair
procedures would require, in respect of a papers-only appeal under s. 43 of the 2015 Act, that
it be in the interests of justice to hold an oral hearing on the appeal, notwithstanding the
statutory default position of a papers-only appeal. There was no dispute between the parties as
to the applicable principles, as summarised in KS.
7

26. I noted in KS that the authorities generally supported the view that oral hearings would
be required in the interests of justice for appeals that would otherwise be papers-only where
demeanour-type grounds for rejecting credibility (such as hesitations, apparent evasions,
failures of recollection or discrepancies (per Cooke J in SUN v Refugee Applications
Commissioner [2013] 2 IR 555 (“SUN”)) had been relied upon.
27. I then stated (at para 58):
“58. It is important to emphasise that an applicant can also be disbelieved (and
therefore be held to lack credibility) on non-demeanour type grounds such as when the
content of his or her story on its own terms lacks coherence or plausibility or contains
material inconsistencies such that a view can be safely arrived at, without the need for
any further oral hearing, that the account should not be accepted.”
28. At para 59, I said the following:
“Again, there is no hard and fast rule and each case will fall to be assessed on its own
facts. There may be a spectrum of cases between “pure” demeanour-type rejection of
personal credibility to cases where an objective assessment of the case advanced by an
applicant on its own terms demonstrates that the case cannot stand up. There may be
cases where there are contested facts in the sense explained by O’Donnell J. in MM
which require an oral hearing to resolve. It is possible to envisage a variety of scenarios
(such as where new issues or evidence going to credibility arises on an appeal) that
can only be fairly tested orally on the appeal or that may depending on the
circumstances be fairly determined on the basis of the papers and written
representations alone. The overriding requirement is that the appellant gets an
opportunity to fairly make his case.”
29. The Tribunal in that section of its decision dealing with a request for an oral hearing,
comprehensively surveys relevant authority over some seven pages. It is accepted fairly by
counsel for the applicant that there was no error of law in this summary of the relevant
authorities. I agree; the Tribunal comprehensively, carefully and fairly summarised the
relevant principles in its assessment of whether or not an oral hearing was required on the facts
of this case.
8

30. The Tribunal having reviewed the relevant authorities and principles held (at paras 1.18
and 1.19) that:
“[1.18] …the Tribunal starts from the position that the International Protection Act,
2015 sets out, as a default, that accelerated appeals proceed without an oral hearing,
and that it is only if the Tribunal is satisfied that it is not in the interests of justice to
proceed on the papers only that this position is altered and an oral hearing held. Thus,
it is not mere convenience or desire for an oral hearing, but whether it is not in the
interests of justice to proceed on papers only that is the statutory test. It is clear from
the legislation and the many decisions of the Supreme Court, the High Court and more
recently the Court of Appeal, that it is not the case that all credibility findings can only
be justly resolved by way of an oral hearing. It is contended that the particular
credibility findings cannot be addressed in the papers, but no meaningful argument as
to why this is so is put forward. There must be engagement and an actual assessment
of the specific credibility grounds in an individual case and there must be an actual
need to hear and test the evidence if it is to be seriously contended that an oral hearing
is necessary in the interests of justice.
[1.19] From this starting point, the Tribunal considers that there are two distinct
aspects of an appeal to be looked at; what the Appellant has advanced or had advanced
on his behalf and whether that satisfies the Tribunal that it is not in the interests of
justice to proceed on the papers only, and second whether the Tribunal, having
independently reviewed the papers, considers that it is not in the interests of justice to
proceed on the papers only.”
31. In my view, these paragraphs are correct in law.
32. Counsel for the applicant submitted that the Tribunal erred in its application of the legal
principles.
33. In truth, the grounds of the applicant's appeal to the Tribunal, as regards his application
for an oral hearing, were in very general terms. It was asserted in these grounds that "The
issues raised by the IPO can only appropriately be dealt with by way of oral evidence and
9

submissions due to the nature of the reasons for refusal" (at para. 2). He asserted that another
reason why an oral hearing was required was that his verbal testimony was the primary
evidence (at para. 4 of grounds of appeal). He says that his reference to the "nature of the
reasons for refusal" was clearly a reference to the fact that the IPO had held that he was
inconsistent, vague and/or non-specific about his claim (grounds of appeal, para. 6).
34. However, tellingly, nowhere in his grounds of appeal did the applicant set out what
additional matters he might want to address in an oral hearing or what issues could not be fairly
assessed on the papers before the Tribunal, given the fact that he had already conveyed his
position orally at the s. 35 interview. As the Tribunal noted in the part of its decision dealing
with the request for an oral hearing, inadequacy of detail is something that can be assessed on
a papers-only basis and it is open to an appellant to provide same in writing (at para. 1.5).
35. While accepting that neither the IPO nor the Tribunal rejected the applicant's credibility
on demeanour-type grounds, such as those identified by Cooke J in SUN and Mac Eochaidh J
in AW (see KS, paras. 52 to 57), counsel for the applicant contended that the basis of the
rejection of the applicant's credibility here was a "neighbour" of demeanour-type grounds for
rejecting credibility, in circumstances where the applicant's evidence was on its own terms
plausible but where it was rejected on the basis that it was, inter alia, lacking in detail and
vague. He submitted that, given the applicant's case that he was exposed to low level but
ongoing discriminatory acts, it was understandable that he would not be able to recall specific
detail of many of these events. Accordingly, it was submitted that his type of case represented
the type of exceptional situation where an oral hearing was required in the interests of justice
to fairly assess his claim on appeal.
36. The applicant did not either in his notice of appeal/grounds of appeal or in his
submissions to the court, specify what additional evidence he would have given at an oral
hearing before the Tribunal on appeal. Indeed, it is difficult to see what additional specific
details or evidence he could have given on his experience given that his evidence at IPO stage
was that he could not remember the detail of most of the discrimination that he alleged that he
experienced.
10

37. Counsel on his behalf submitted that he would have been able to more effectively
convey a sense of the lived experience of the low-level discrimination he suffered if he was
able to give oral evidence before the Tribunal member.
38. In my view, the circumstances here are not sufficient to elevate this case into the class
of case where the default rule of a papers-only appeal should be disapplied. In my view, there
is force in the submission made by counsel for the respondents to the effect that, in substance,
the applicant would simply be seeking to restate the evidence he gave before the IPO. I
particularly have regard to the fact that, at the s. 35 interview, the IPO officer put to him that
his account was vague and non-specific and gave him an opportunity to provide more detail. I
also take into account the fact that, in his grounds of appeal, he did not spell out any additional
evidence or material which he wanted the Tribunal to have regard to. While it is
understandable why he may have preferred to have a face-to-face encounter with the Tribunal
member, that is not the statutory default position, and I do not see that there are sufficient
circumstances here on the facts to reach a finding that the Tribunal erred in law in failing to
provide the applicant with an oral hearing.
39. Accordingly, I believe that the Tribunal acted lawfully when it held (at paras 1.20 and
1.26):
“[1.20] In terms of the first part, the Tribunal considers that there are no new
elements that require to be heard, no issues have been raised about the conduct of the
interview of misinterpretation of any particular fact which requires clarification and
the issues advanced are capable of being advanced on the papers. The credibility
findings in this case are capable of objective assessment on the papers and are not
based on subjective factors arising from the oral hearing such as demeanour type
findings. Submissions as to why those findings are not correct were made and the
Tribunal can adequately assess these points on the papers. He has had an opportunity
to set out any additional information or clarification in his notice of appeal but has
not done so.”
“[1.26] Thus the Tribunal concludes from the above that the only purpose that would
be served by an oral hearing is for the Appellant to restate his evidence. The
submissions as to why there should be an oral hearing are systemic arguments
11

against the concept of safe countries and the legislative requirement that the Tribunal
determine the case on the papers unless it is satisfied that it is not in the interests of
justice to do so. They do not engage with specifics of this particular case. The
Tribunal considers that the issue raised in relation to the findings in the section 39
reports are matters which can be addressed by way of written submissions. Further,
in G.A v I.P.A.T [2022] IEHC 440 the Honourable Mr. Justice Heslin found that the
Tribunal does not have to stop its deliberations and invite further observations or
submissions from the Appellant. Therefore, the Tribunal is satisfied that it has all the
information and submissions that are forthcoming from the Appellant and his reason
for seeking an oral hearing i.e. that he wants to address the credibility issues has
been addressed by his having the opportunity to address these matters in his notice of
appeal and submissions.”
40. In my view, it was reasonably open to the Tribunal on the case before it to conclude
that "The only purpose that would be served by an oral hearing is for the appellant to restate
his evidence". The Tribunal's conclusion that the submissions made in the grounds of appeal
did not engage with the specifics of the case was also a finding open to it (para. [1.26]).
41. The Tribunal, in discharge of its independent duty to assess whether there were any
independent reasons which required in the interests of justice that an oral hearing be held, found
as follows (at paras. 1.27 to 1.29):
“[1.27] Separately, the Tribunal has an independent duty to review the claim and
assess whether there are any independent reasons which require, in the interests of
justice, that an oral hearing be held. The Tribunal considers that the Appellant was
given an adequate opportunity to set out his claim his Questionnaire and his
Interview, that he was assisted by interpreters during this process and that there is no
procedural unfairness about any of this.
[1.28] The Tribunal also does not consider that there are areas of his claims that
fairness requires to be probed further or that he has not been given the opportunity to
adequately address. Having independently reviewed the papers, the Tribunal
considers that if an oral hearing was granted, it would be for no purpose other than
for the Appellant to simply restate his claim. The Tribunal does not consider that
12

there are issues that ought to be put to him for further clarity, nor indeed that there is
anything ambiguous in relation to his claim that needs to be clarified for the Tribunal
to carry out its functions.
[1.29] For the same reasons as set out above, the Tribunal does not consider that
depriving him of the opportunity to restate that which has already been said is
contrary to the interests of justice. The Tribunal has borne this is mind throughout its
assessment of the case and has always borne in mind the possibility of directing an
oral hearing if such became necessary on any particular point. No such point arose
during the Tribunal’s assessment of the Appeal.”
42. In my view, there was no legal error in these findings and it was reasonably open to the
Tribunal to reach these findings on the present state of the law.
43. Accordingly, in my view, there is no basis to quash the decision of the Tribunal to
refuse the applicant an oral hearing in this matter.
Other Grounds of Challenge to the Decision
44. I will turn now to the remaining issues arising on the applicant’s case as argued before
me. Before I do so, it is useful to deal with a number of the other grounds which were advanced
in the applicant’s statement of grounds, but which were not pursued or pressed with any vigour
at the hearing before me. These grounds essentially related to a contention that the applicant's
right to fair procedures was breached by the failure of the Tribunal to put particular intended
credibility findings to the applicant in advance of the Tribunal’s decision to invite the
applicant's views on same.
Fair procedures required Tribunal to revert to applicant with certain proposed findings?
45. Accordingly, the applicant claimed that the Tribunal acted in breach of fair procedures
in holding that, if the applicant was genuinely in fear of persecution in Georgia, he would have
persisted with his application for international protection in the UK, without putting that
assessment to the applicant in advance. However, this matter was clearly put to the applicant
in the IPO process, during the s. 35 interview, and, indeed, it was specifically pointed out that
13

the circumstances of him not persisting with an application for protection in the UK could cast
doubt on his credibility (s.35 interview, questions 44 to 48).
46. As made clear by Peart J in his judgment in the Court of Appeal in BW v Refugee
Appeals Tribunal [2018] 2 ILRM 56 at 72-73, the requirement that the applicant must be given
a fair opportunity of addressing a concern as to credibility on the part of the decision maker,
before any adverse finding of credibility is made against him, is fulfilled where the particular
issue was raised at first instance during the [IPO] process. It is well-established that the
Tribunal is not obliged to revert to an applicant where it intends to make an adverse credibility
finding arising from his own evidence early in the process: see Mac Eochaidh J in MA v Refugee
Appeals Tribunal [2015] IEHC 528 at para. 20 (as discussed in my judgment in KS at paras 68
to 72).
47. A similar case was made in the statement of grounds that the applicant, as a matter of
fair procedures, was entitled to be invited by the Tribunal in advance of its decision to comment
on a potentially adverse finding on the destruction of the applicant's passport. Again, this was
not pressed at the hearing before me and sensibly so in circumstances where the question of
the circumstances of the destruction of the applicant's passport was put to the applicant both in
his s. 35 interview and was the subject of an adverse credibility finding in the IPO’s s. 39
report. These adverse credibility assessments – both of the IPO and of the Tribunal –
exclusively flow from the applicant's own direct evidence.
48. Another ground contained in the applicant’s statement of grounds was that it was a
breach of fair procedures on the part of the Tribunal to fail to put its assessment of the
insufficiency of supporting documentation to the applicant in advance of its decision. As
Heslin J held in GA v IPAT [2022] IEHC 440, an applicant is always on notice of the necessity
to produce as much supporting documentation as possible. This is made abundantly clear in,
inter alia, the IPO questionnaire document filled out by the applicant. In that judgment, Heslin
J. quoted in extensive detail from the IPO questionnaire provided to applicants for international
protection and noted the extent to which that questionnaire emphasises the need for applicants
to submit all relevant documents. I do not see that any breach of fair procedures arises in the
circumstances.
Adverse Reliance by Tribunal on applicant’s destruction of his passport
14

49. In relation to the passport issue, the only case made on the applicant's pleadings was a
breach of fair procedures in relation to the alleged failure by the Tribunal to revert to him on
the question of an adverse credibility finding arising from his destruction of his passport, which
I have dealt with above. At the hearing before me, the applicant also sought to make
submissions on the inappropriateness of the Tribunal drawing adverse inferences from the fact
of the applicant's destruction of his passport. As this ground is not pleaded, I do not think it
can be validly advanced by the applicant. In any event, in my view, no error in this regard can
be made out.
50. Counsel for the applicant sought to contend at the hearing before me that it was
disproportionate of the Tribunal to draw an adverse inference from the destruction by the
applicant of his passport in London on the basis that such destruction was not inherently at
odds with his claim to be a legitimate refugee as, for example, the destruction of the passport
might be consistent with seeking to avoid or delay being returned to the country of origin where
the applicant claimed he would be exposed to a risk of persecution. However, it was clearly
open to the Tribunal on the particular evidence before it to take the view that the intentional
destruction by the applicant of his passport was relevant to assessing his credibility. The
applicant stated, in his s. 35 interview, that he sought to throw away his passport when he was
approaching the relevant officials in the airport in London. This was in circumstances where
he had earlier in the interview claimed that he did not use a passport to get to London. He in
fact took a photograph of the personal details page of the passport which he subsequently used
in an application for workforce access. This was not a situation where, on his own evidence,
he destroyed his passport with a view to protecting his safety or for some other objectively
justifiable reason.
51. As noted by the respondents in their submissions, s. 20(1)(f) of the 2015 Act empowers
an immigration officer or a member of An Garda Síochána to arrest and detain an applicant for
international protection who, without reasonable excuse, has destroyed his or her identity or
travel document. This power appears to reflect the determination of the UNHCR Executive
Committee Declaration on the Problem of Refugees and Asylum-Seekers Who Move in an
Irregular Manner from a Country in Which They Had Already Found Protection, No. 58 (XL)
– 1989, where the Committee stated (cited in Prof Goodwin-Gill, Article 31 of the 1951
15

Convention relating to the Status of Refugees: Non-penalization, Detention and Protection
(UNHCR, 2001) at p. 68):
The problem of irregular movements is compounded by the use, by a growing
number of refugees and asylum-seekers, of fraudulent documentation and their
practice of wilfully destroying or disposing of travel and/or other documents in
order to mislead the authorities of their country of arrival. These practices
complicate the personal identification of the person concerned and the
determination of the country where he stayed prior to arrival, and the nature
and duration of his stay in such a country. Practices of this kind are fraudulent
and may weaken the case of the person concerned…”
52. While of course there may be circumstances in which there is a reasonable or legitimate
excuse or justification for an IP applicant destroying their passport, in my view it was perfectly
open to the Tribunal on the evidence before it in this case to draw adverse inferences from the
applicant’s destruction of his passport which occurred while he was approaching an
immigration officer in an airport in London.
Alleged mischaracterisation of basis for applicant’s international protection claim
53. The applicant claims that the Tribunal fundamentally mischaracterised the basis of his
claim for international protection such as to vitiate the Tribunal’s decision. He submits that the
COI made clear that those perceived to be Russian, or Russian sympathisers, by virtue of being
Russian speakers in Georgia, were subject to discrimination in Georgia but that the Tribunal
mis-framed his claim as one of being an ethnic Georgian who spoke Russian thereby
discounting relevant COI which supported his actual case which was that as a Russian speaking
Georgian he would be assumed to be a Russia-sympathiser and would be discriminated against
as such.
54. I do not think that this contention is borne out by the actual terms of the Tribunal’s
decision.
55. The Tribunal's decision, in its first paragraph (at para. [1.1]), stated that the applicant
was a 22-year-old man from Georgia who made a claim for protection "on the basis that he is
16

a Russian speaker and was discriminated against". Under the heading "Outline of claim", at
para. [2.1] of the decision, the Tribunal records the applicant's claim before it as, in essence,
being "that he is a Russian speaker and some people consider him to support Russia". It is
clear therefore that the Tribunal understood the basis of the applicant’s claim.
56. The Tribunal stated (at para. [4.7] of its decision) that:-
"The IPO reviewed the COI and put to the appellant that the was no COI to support his
claim and that Russian is commonly spoken in Georgia. In reply he was vague, and
suggested that those [there] are people who are over 35 years old but young people
wouldn't understand it. The Tribunal has reviewed the COI on file and cannot see any
obvious support for the proposition that Georgian nationals who speak Russian as their
primary language are discriminated against. His legal representative submits that his
claim is consistent and relies upon two news articles. The first is from the Moscow
Times. This article suggests that Russian citizens in a cafe when asked to confirm they
don't support Putin before being served. It goes on to state that some people are asked
not to speak Russian or discuss politics when drunk and some nightclubs or other
services refuse service to Russian citizens or banks who requested that they confirm
their position before doing business with them."
57. The decision then goes on to state, at para. [4.8], as follows:-
"The appellant is not a Russian citizen but is a Georgian citizen who speaks
Russian. He had a Georgian passport to prove this until he voluntarily destroyed it,
and had it at all times in Georgia. He has also lived in Georgia from 2007 until he left,
and attended school there, so there is an air of unreality to the suggestion that he would
be treated as a Russian citizen. A link to an article from the Carnegie Endowment did
not appear to work. The appellant's legal representative was contacted to provide the
correct link but nothing was provided. The Tribunal notes that there are other news
articles, including from the Carnegie Endowment about ethnic Russians facing
discrimination, but the Tribunal was unable to locate anything in relation to ethnic
Georgians who speak Russian being discriminated against. On balance, therefore, the
Tribunal finds that while there is COI to suggest discrimination against Russian citizens
17

and nationals in Georgia, there was no COI to support the appellant's claim that he, as
an ethnic Georgian who speaks Russian, is discriminated against."
58. The applicant contends that this this is an inappropriate distinction. The key point of
his claim is that his status as a Russian speaker left him open to the assumption that he was
either Russian or sympathetic to Russia. He submits that this, in essence, was the Moscow
Times article conveyed. The applicant says that while the Tribunal correctly characterised his
claim in paras. 1.1 and 2.1 as set out above, it then fell into error subsequently. At para. 4.10
of its decision, the Tribunal holds that, on the balance of probabilities, "The only material
element of his claim which is accepted is his Georgian nationality". It does not spell out that
in fact it was accepted that he was a Russian speaker. He submits that, at para. 7.3 of the
decision, relating to his claim for subsidiary protection, the Tribunal doubles down on this
mistake by holding that there is no reason to consider that Georgian citizens in Georgia are at
risk of torture, inhuman or degrading treatment, by state or non-state actors, by reason of their
nationality. He submits that this makes clear that the Tribunal simply failed to engage with the
core base of his claim. His claim was, in essence, that, on account of him being a Russian
speaker, he was seen as ethnically Russian in Georgia, thereby leaving him exposed to
discrimination.
59. I cannot hold that the Tribunal did not have any reasonable basis to make the findings
it did in relation to the nature of his claim. The only article with which the appellant put before
the Tribunal was that from the Moscow Times. While that article was not before me in
evidence, there was no evidence that the Tribunal's characterisation of this article in its decision
as referencing the ill-treatment of Russian citizens in Georgia was in error. As is clear from
the terms of the s. 35 questionnaire and the IPO recommendation decision, Russian is widely
spoken in Georgia by many Georgian citizens and nationals. The evidence accordingly before
the Tribunal was that Russian is a very common language in Georgia. The Tribunal set out, in
para. 4.8 of its decision (as set out above), that there were other news articles about ethnic
Russians facing discrimination. Again, there is no evidence before me that that was a statement
without any foundation in the evidence before the Tribunal.
60. I do not accept that, reading the Tribunal's decision in the round, it did not evaluate the
applicant's case on the basis that he was not a Russian speaker or that it did not appreciate his
case that he was being discriminated against because as a Russian speaker he might be assumed
18

to be a Russian sympathiser. Rather, the Tribunal took the view that there was a material
difference on the evidence and materials before it, between Russian citizens in Georgia and
how they were treated as such, and Russian-speaking ethnic Georgians. On the face of it, I do
not see that it was irrational to make a distinction between ethnic Russians and Russian citizens
getting unfavourable treatment in Georgia, on the one hand, and Georgian citizens speaking
Russian in Georgia, on the other, particularly where the applicant accepted that Russian is very
commonly spoken in Georgia. In the circumstances, I cannot hold that the findings were not
grounded in the evidence before the Tribunal or were otherwise unlawful.
Alleged errors in relying on absence of documentation and application of s.28(7) 2015 Act
61. At para 4.3 of its decision, the Tribunal set out the terms of s.28(7) of the 2015 Act as
follows:
“Where aspects of the applicant’s statements are not supported by documentary or
other evidence, those aspects shall not need confirmation where the international
protection officer or, as the case may be, the Tribunal, is satisfied that—
(a) the applicant has made a genuine effort to substantiate his or her application,
(b) all relevant elements at the applicant’s disposal have been submitted and a
satisfactory explanation regarding any lack of other relevant elements has been given,
(c) the applicant’s statements are found to be coherent and plausible and do not run
counter to available specific and general information relevant to the applicant’s case,
(d) the applicant has applied for international protection at the earliest possible time,
unless the applicant can demonstrate good reason for not having done so, and
(e) the general credibility of the applicant has been established.”
62. The Tribunal then noted (at para 4.4) that there were no supporting documents in
relation to the material elements of the applicant’s claim noting he hadn't provided documents
about his time living in Russia nor did he provide his original passport. It concluded that that
there are documents which he could have obtained with reasonable effort, if his claim was
credible, and that this was not a claim where the absence of documentation was understandable
or reasonable.
19

63. The applicant sought to contend that the Tribunal erred in the manner in which it
considered s. 28(7) of the 2015 Act by relying on the absence of documentation to make
adverse findings of credibility, when the statutory scheme requires that an applicant's general
credibility be considered prior to any consideration of an absence of documentation.
64. It is clear that the “benefit of the doubt” provision in s. 28(7) of the 2015 Act is only
engaged where the several criteria set out in the section are met. In particular, it has been
emphasised that the “benefit of the doubt” will only be engaged where the general credibility
of the applicant has already been established. As I noted in my judgment in AH v IPAT [2022]
IEHC 84 at para 17:
In short, it is clear that before the benefit of the doubt can be given in relation
to undocumented aspects of an applicant’s claims, the applicant’s general
credibility must be established (see s.28(7)(e)). Once the applicant’s general
credibility has been established, undocumented aspects of the applicant’s case
do not need to be confirmed i.e. can get the benefit of the doubt where, but only
where, the four other factors in s. 28 (7)(a) to (d) are satisfied.
65. I do not see that the Tribunal erred in law in its consideration of the question of the
absence of supporting documentation or in its application of s. 28(7). The conclusion reached
by the Tribunal that this was not a claim where the absence of documentation was
understandable or reasonable was open to it on the evidence (or lack of evidence) before it.
More to the point, the Tribunal in any event went on to consider in some detail why the
applicant’s claim lacked credibility on the basis of an evaluation of his evidence and the
applicable COI.
Conclusion
66. For the reasons outlined above, I conclude that the applicant is not entitled to the relief
he claims.
20
